reasoning: Iteration 2 delivered a strong 16% improvement with correct results (164.4ms → 137.9ms). GenDB is now 1.6x slower than DuckDB best (86.4ms), with scan_filter dominating at 64.5ms (46.8% of total). The final iteration should leverage proven, low-risk I/O optimizations (thread tuning and madvise removal) that differ from Iteration 1's failed CPU/filter approach. Expected combined impact: 15-35ms reduction (11-25% improvement), bringing total to ~107-123ms.
focus_areas: Reduce thread count from 64 to 8-16 for HDD-optimal sequential access
Increase morsel size from 100K to 500K rows to reduce random seeks
Remove MADV_SEQUENTIAL hint that conflicts with parallel HDD access
strategy_notes: This is the final iteration (remaining=0), so use the budget decisively on high-impact, proven optimizations. Priority 1 and 2 (indices 0, 1) target the dominant I/O bottleneck with LOW risk. These are fundamentally different from Iteration 1's CPU-focused approach (which failed on precision). The combination of reduced thread count, larger morsels, and madvise removal directly addresses HDD thrashing caused by 64 threads creating excessive random seeks. Thread count reduction from 64 to 8-16 aligns with HDD bandwidth characteristics (sequential I/O benefits more from few large threads than many small threads). Expected total time after optimization: 107-123ms (vs. current 137.9ms), closing the gap to DuckDB significantly. If improvements materialize as expected, next iteration (if budget allowed) would target AVX-512 SIMD upgrade (Priority 3) for additional 3-8ms gain.
notes: Both recommendations target HDD I/O efficiency and have explicit, low-risk code changes (lines 163-164 for thread/morsel params, line 86 for madvise). No architectural changes required. Expected improvement is substantial (15-35ms = 11-25%) based on elimination of HDD thrashing from excessive parallelism and conflicting kernel read-ahead. This is aggressive but well-justified given positive Iteration 2 trajectory and concrete bottleneck diagnosis.
